ReleaseLabel: dabbler-ai-orchestration 0.43.0 local VSIX (Set 100 Session 2 of 2 — module row lifecycle actions + Add-module scaffolding; no Marketplace publish, single release boundary after Set 101)
Notes: Session 1's three walks (below) cover the flattened tree + kind chips and still apply unchanged. Session 2 adds Walks 4-6, which are the only walks that need a FRESH install of the Session 2 VSIX — the interactive parts of Add/Rename/Delete (VS Code's native input boxes and confirm dialogs) are NOT driven by the Layer 3 Playwright suite this session (clicking those buttons in an automated run would open a blocking modal Playwright never dismisses), so these three walks are where a human's click is the ONLY verification the row-level lifecycle actions get end-to-end. UAT is SUGGESTED and per-session — optional, does not gate close. Install ONLY the local VSIX built from this working tree (tools/dabbler-ai-orchestration/dabbler-ai-orchestration-0.43.0.vsix); do not publish it. Walk order: Walk 1 then Walk 2 in THIS repository (dabbler-ai-orchestration), then Walk 3, 4, 5, 6 in a fresh scratch folder (Walks 4-6 build on the same scratch folder Walk 3 creates). No steps are skipped or out of order.

Review.Subarea: Walk 1 — status buckets sit directly under the module row (this repo)
Review.ItemLabel: The Plan / Session sets child rows are gone; buckets are the module's direct children
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: a fresh install, about to look at the Work Explorer tree in THIS repository (dabbler-ai-orchestration), which has many session sets and no docs/modules.yaml, so it renders a single muted 'Default' module.
1. In this repository's VS Code window, install the local file tools/dabbler-ai-orchestration/dabbler-ai-orchestration-0.43.0.vsix (Extensions view -> '...' menu -> 'Install from VSIX...'), then run 'Developer: Reload Window'.
2. Click the Dabbler AI Orchestration icon in the Activity Bar to open the Work Explorer.
3. Find the single, visually muted 'Default' row at the top of the tree.
4. Read the rows immediately indented beneath 'Default', top to bottom.
5. Look for any row labeled 'Plan' or 'Session sets' anywhere in the tree.
Review.Expectation: Immediately beneath the 'Default' module row are the uppercase status-bucket headers themselves — 'IN PROGRESS (N)', 'NOT STARTED (N)', 'COMPLETE (N)' (and 'CANCELLED (N)' if any cancelled sets exist) — each with its session-set rows one indent level deeper. There is NO row labeled 'Plan' and NO row labeled 'Session sets' anywhere in the tree: the two child rows that sat between the module and its buckets in 0.41.x/0.42.x are gone, so the tree is exactly three levels deep (module, bucket, set row).
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Layer 3 session-sets-tree.spec.ts asserts module-pseudo-default-plan and module-pseudo-default-session-sets have count 0, the three buckets sit directly inside the module body (aria-level 2), and rows sit at aria-level 3; Layer 2 moduleTier source scans pin the retired render functions out of client.js.

Review.Subarea: Walk 2 — arrow keys reach buckets directly; a bucket collapses independently (this repo)
Review.ItemLabel: Down-arrow from the module lands on the first bucket; Enter folds one bucket while the module and sibling buckets stay visible
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: still in THIS repository's Work Explorer, immediately after Walk 1, with the 'Default' module expanded.
1. Click once on the 'Default' module row. NOTE: this click also COLLAPSES the module (its chevron flips to a right-triangle) — that is expected; the click gives the tree keyboard focus.
2. Press the Right arrow key once to re-expand the 'Default' module (its chevron flips back to a down-triangle).
3. Press the Down arrow key once and note which row is highlighted.
4. With that bucket header highlighted (e.g. 'IN PROGRESS'), press Enter once.
5. Look at the session-set rows that were nested under that bucket.
6. Look at whether the 'Default' row and the OTHER bucket headers are still visible.
7. Press Enter again on the same bucket header.
Review.Expectation: The initial click collapses 'Default' and the Right arrow re-expands it — the whole exchange happens on the module row with no mouse travel. Pressing Down from 'Default' then highlights the first status-bucket header directly (e.g. 'IN PROGRESS (N)') — there is no intermediate 'Plan' or 'Session sets' stop. Pressing Enter on the bucket collapses it: its chevron flips from a down-triangle to a right-triangle and ONLY that bucket's session-set rows disappear. The 'Default' module row and the other bucket headers REMAIN visible. Pressing Enter again re-expands the bucket and its rows reappear.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Layer 3 module-tier.spec.ts drives the bucket-header click and Enter/ArrowLeft/ArrowRight toggles, asserting aria-expanded flips, the row hides, and the module stays expanded; the shared roving-tabindex handler covers arrow navigation.

Review.FunctionalArea: Kind-aware rows — chips and the plan gate
Review.Subarea: Walk 3 — plan/decomposition chips render; the decomposition row carries the blocked marker until its plan set completes (scratch repo)
Review.ItemLabel: A 'kind: plan' set shows a small 'plan' chip; a 'kind: decomposition' set shows a 'decomposition' chip plus the chain blocked-marker whose tooltip names the incomplete plan set
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: about to create a tiny throwaway workspace so you can see the kind chips and the prerequisite gate that replaced the old 'blocked - add a plan first' row.
1. Create a new empty folder, e.g. C:\temp\dabbler-100-uat.
2. Inside it create the file docs\modules.yaml with EXACTLY this content:
modules:
  - slug: alpha
    title: Alpha
3. Create the file docs\session-sets\001-alpha-plan\spec.md with EXACTLY this content:
# 001-alpha-plan

## Session Set Configuration
```yaml
tier: full
requiresUAT: false
requiresE2E: false
module: alpha
kind: plan
```
4. Create the file docs\session-sets\002-alpha-decomposition\spec.md with EXACTLY this content:
# 002-alpha-decomposition

## Session Set Configuration
```yaml
tier: full
requiresUAT: false
requiresE2E: false
module: alpha
kind: decomposition
prerequisites:
  - slug: 001-alpha-plan
    condition: complete
```
5. Open that folder in a new VS Code window that has the 0.43.0 VSIX installed, and open the Work Explorer.
6. Expand the 'Alpha' module and its 'NOT STARTED (2)' bucket.
7. Read the '001-alpha-plan' row, including any small chip after its name.
8. Read the '002-alpha-decomposition' row, including any small chip and any extra glyph after its name.
9. Hover the mouse over the small chain glyph on the '002-alpha-decomposition' row and read the tooltip.
Review.Expectation: Both sets appear under the 'Alpha' module's 'NOT STARTED (2)' bucket. The '001-alpha-plan' row shows a small rounded chip reading exactly 'plan' right after its name, and NO chain glyph. The '002-alpha-decomposition' row shows a chip reading exactly 'decomposition' AND a small chain glyph after its name. Hovering the chain glyph shows a tooltip reading 'Blocked by prerequisites: 001-alpha-plan (not started) — all must complete first.' — the gate that used to render as a 'blocked - add a plan first' child row now rides the decomposition set itself. Hovering a chip explains the lifecycle set (e.g. 'Module lifecycle set: creates or imports this module's project plan.').
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Layer 2 moduleLifecycleUi.test.ts pins the scan->badge round-trip, the state matrix, and the scaffolded-decomposition blocked marker + tooltip text (including the unblock when the plan set completes); Layer 3 module-tier.spec.ts pins the rendered chip text and title attribute.

Review.FunctionalArea: Module row lifecycle actions — the strip's new/retired buttons
Review.Subarea: Walk 4 — a declared module's strip shows Open Plan / Add / Rename... / Delete...; the old AI Plan / Import Plan / AI Sets buttons are gone
Review.ItemLabel: Hovering the 'Alpha' module row reveals exactly four buttons, none of them the old AI Plan / Import Plan / AI Sets
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: continuing in the SAME scratch folder Walk 3 created (C:\temp\dabbler-100-uat), with the Session 2 VSIX installed and 'Developer: Reload Window' already run.
1. In the Work Explorer, hover the mouse over the 'Alpha' module row's header text (not a bucket, not a session-set row).
2. Read every button that appears to the right of the module name.
3. Right-click the 'Alpha' module row header instead, and read the menu that pops up.
4. Press Escape to close that menu.
Review.Expectation: Hovering reveals exactly four buttons in this order: 'Open Plan', 'Add', 'Rename…', 'Delete…'. There is NO 'AI Plan' button, NO 'Import Plan' button, and NO 'AI Sets' button anywhere in the strip. The right-click menu shows the same four items ('Open Plan', 'Add Module…', 'Rename Module…', 'Delete Module…') and nothing else.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Layer 3 module-tier.spec.ts asserts the declared strip has exactly 4 `.module-action` buttons with `data-module-action` values open-plan/add-module/rename-module/delete-module; Layer 2 moduleLifecycleUi.test.ts source-scans client.js and CustomSessionSetsView.ts confirming ai-plan/import-plan/ai-sets are absent from both the strip and the context menu.

Review.FunctionalArea: Module row lifecycle actions — Add module scaffolds its lifecycle sets
Review.Subarea: Walk 5 — clicking Add on the Alpha row declares a brand-new module whose two lifecycle sets appear plan=ready / decomposition=blocked
Review.ItemLabel: Add prompts for a new slug/title, then the new module and its two sets show up in the tree without a manual refresh
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: same scratch folder, Work Explorer still open.
1. Hover the 'Alpha' module row and click the 'Add' button.
2. In the input box titled 'New module (1/2): slug', type: bravo — then press Enter.
3. In the input box titled 'New module (2/2): display title', type: Bravo — then press Enter.
4. Read the notification toast that appears in the bottom-right corner.
5. Look at the Work Explorer tree for a new 'Bravo' module row; expand it and its 'NOT STARTED' bucket.
6. Read the two new session-set rows' names and chips.
Review.Expectation: A new 'Bravo' module row appears in the tree (no manual refresh needed) with a project-plan.md editor tab opened for it. The toast names both scaffolded sets, e.g. 'Module "bravo" appended to docs/modules.yaml. Plan stub created at docs/modules/bravo/project-plan.md — fill it in, then decompose it into session sets. Next steps scaffolded: 00N-bravo-plan and 00N-bravo-decomposition.' Under 'Bravo' -> 'NOT STARTED', two rows appear: one named '...-bravo-plan' with a 'plan' chip and no chain glyph, and one named '...-bravo-decomposition' with a 'decomposition' chip AND a chain glyph (hovering it names the plan set as the unmet prerequisite) — the same ready/blocked pairing Walk 3 showed for Alpha.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: moduleAuthoring.test.ts (runNewModuleFlow lifecycle-scaffold tests) pins the manifest append + scaffoldModuleLifecycleSets wiring and the combined toast wording end-to-end against the real filesystem; s2-dogfood.md records the same scaffold + plan-gate outcome run directly against the compiled writer.

Review.FunctionalArea: Module row lifecycle actions — Rename and Delete, end to end
Review.Subarea: Walk 6 — renaming Bravo regroups its two sets with zero orphans; deleting it removes the manifest entry and both unstarted scaffolds
Review.ItemLabel: Rename… and Delete… on the Bravo row run without ever asking WHICH module — only the new slug/title and a confirm
Review.HumanAction:
WHERE YOU ARE: same scratch folder, right after Walk 5 (a 'Bravo' module with two lifecycle sets exists).
1. Hover the 'Bravo' module row and click 'Rename…'.
2. In the slug input box (pre-filled 'bravo'), replace it with: charlie — then press Enter.
3. In the title input box (pre-filled 'Bravo'), replace it with: Charlie — then press Enter.
4. Read the confirm dialog that appears, then click its 'Rename Module' button.
5. Confirm the tree now shows 'Charlie' with the same two sets underneath, still one 'plan' + one 'decomposition' chip.
6. Hover the 'Charlie' module row and click 'Delete…'.
7. Read the confirm dialog, then click its 'Delete Module' button.
8. Confirm the 'Charlie' module row is gone from the tree entirely.
Review.Expectation: Step 1-3: no module-picker QuickPick ever appears — the flow goes straight from the click to the slug input box, because the row already carries which module. Step 4's confirm dialog names both sets being restamped (e.g. '...-bravo-plan, ...-bravo-decomposition') and the toast after confirming reads success. Step 5: 'Charlie' now groups the two sets (still named with their original '...-bravo-...' filenames — only the module: stamp changed, not the set names), still showing one 'plan' + one 'decomposition' chip. Step 6-7: same no-QuickPick behavior; the confirm dialog states the manifest entry will be removed and both sets — being unstarted lifecycle scaffolds with no work done — will be removed outright (not merely cancelled). Step 8: 'Charlie' disappears from the tree completely; the sets it held are gone too (not relocated to 'Default'/'Unassigned').
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: renameModule.test.ts and deleteModule.test.ts (preselectedSlug suites) pin that a row/context invocation skips pickModule and resolves the carried slug directly; s2-dogfood.md records the identical rename-then-delete sequence run against the real compiled writers with zero orphans and full removal.
